cases.answer: Well-formedness is grammatical: 2+2=5 is a legal equation, merely false. Provability PA⊢P is syntactic (a finite derivation exists); truth ℕ⊨P is semantic (evaluation in the standard model). Different relations. ∀+=x))0 is malformed, so no truth value and not provable. 2+2=4 is well-formed, provable in PA, true in ℕ. 2+2=5 is well-formed, not provable in consistent PA, false in ℕ. G_PA is well-formed, not provable in PA (if PA is consistent), yet true in ℕ (if PA is sound). So the three predicates only coincide on easy sentences.

⊢ is about derivations in PA, ⊨ is about truth in ℕ — separate relations. Malformed strings like ∀+=x))0 aren't true or false at all. 2+2=4 is well-formed, provable, and true; 2+2=5 is well-formed and false but not provable; the Gödel sentence is well-formed and true in ℕ but PA can't prove it (assuming consistency). Provable and true agree on simple cases but the Gödel sentence shows they differ.

Well-formed means it parses; 2+2=5 parses but is false. Provability is a finite proof in PA; truth is evaluation in the natural numbers. They are different. The malformed string has no truth value. 2+2=4 is provable and true; 2+2=5 is false and unprovable; the Gödel sentence is true but PA doesn't prove it. So truth is broader than provability.
A sentence is true exactly when PA can prove it, and provable exactly when it's true — same thing. 2+2=4 is true because PA proves it. The Gödel sentence is true and PA proves it too.
∀+=x))0 is just a false statement. 2+2=5 is also false. Well-formed sentences are the true ones, and the badly written ones are the false ones. Provable means we wrote it correctly.
The Gödel sentence is the liar paradox: it just says 'this sentence is false', which is why it breaks math. It isn't really an arithmetic statement, it's an English self-reference trick.
Gödel proved some things are true but unprovable. That's incompleteness. Truth and proof are different. It's very deep and shows the limits of mathematics.
Peano arithmetic was developed by Giuseppe Peano in the 19th century and is very important. Numbers are interesting and addition is commutative. I like math.
